planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. All parts of White Baneberry are highly toxic — plant away from areas accessible to children and pets.

directions: White Baneberry, known as Doll's Eyes for its striking porcelain-white berries with a dark purple dot, is a bold native woodland perennial, native to rich, moist, deciduous forests throughout eastern North America from Nova Scotia to Georgia and west to Oklahoma, where it thrives in the deep shade of sugar maple, beech, and basswood forests. Fluffy white flower spikes appear in spring, followed by the distinctive white berries on thick, red-stalked clusters — the berries persist into fall and are visually spectacular, though all parts of the plant are highly toxic and the berries are potentially lethal. An excellent bold-textured foliage plant for the shade garden, with large, deeply divided compound leaves that provide attractive green structure from spring through fall dormancy; plant in deeply amended, moist, humus-rich soil and allow it to slowly spread into colonies. Never ingest any part of this plant.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. All parts of Red Baneberry are poisonous — plant away from areas frequented by children and pets.

directions: Red Baneberry is a native woodland perennial closely related to White Baneberry (Actaea pachypoda) and similarly arresting in fruit — clusters of brilliant, glossy red berries on slender stems ripen in late summer and persist into fall, providing vivid color in the shade garden at a time when most woodland plants are fading. Native to moist, rich, deciduous and mixed forests throughout much of North America, from sea level to mountain slopes, Red Baneberry blooms in spring with fluffy white flower spikes similar to those of its cousin before producing its distinctive red (occasionally white) berries that are highly poisonous to humans but consumed by some birds without apparent harm. Grow in consistently moist, deeply amended, humus-rich soil in full to partial shade; the large, compound, deeply lobed leaves provide bold texture throughout the growing season. Keep away from children and pets — all parts of this plant are toxic if ingested.

planting.note: Divide rhizome clumps in fall or plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Yellow Mandarin slowly spreads by rhizome to form colonies in the moist woodland garden.

directions: Yellow Mandarin is an elegant native woodland perennial, native to rich, moist, deciduous forests of eastern North America from Ontario south to Georgia and Alabama, with arching stems bearing alternating, broadly oval, clasping leaves and pendant, creamy-yellow, bell-shaped flowers dangling from branch tips in April and May — an effect reminiscent of Solomon's seal but with larger, more distinctly yellow-green flowers. The small, red to orange berries that follow in late summer are attractive and eaten by some birds, adding late-season interest to the shaded garden. Yellow Mandarin spreads slowly by rhizome to form loose, graceful colonies and is one of the most architecturally pleasing of the native spring woodland wildflowers, providing strong vertical structure in layers under taller shade trees. Divide clumps in fall, or plant nursery transplants in spring in well-amended, consistently moist, humus-rich soil.

planting.note: Divide rhizome clumps in fall or plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Nodding Mandarin is less common in cultivation than Yellow Mandarin but adapts well to the same moist woodland garden conditions.

directions: Nodding Mandarin is a beautiful native woodland perennial distinguished from its close relative Yellow Mandarin (Disporum lanuginosum) by its larger, pure white, purple-spotted, bell-shaped flowers that dangle conspicuously from arching branch tips in April and May — an unusual and eye-catching display in the spring shade garden. Native to moist, rich, deciduous cove forests and north-facing slopes of the Appalachians from Ohio and Pennsylvania south to Georgia and Alabama, it grows in similar conditions to Solomon's seal and False Solomon's seal, with which it often associates. The red-orange berries that follow in summer attract birds and add late-season interest; the plant slowly spreads by rhizome to form loose, graceful colonies over time. Divide clumps in fall, or plant nursery transplants in spring in deeply amended, consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full shade.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States (Weakley)

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; honewort self-sows freely and can naturalize in the shade garden. A perennial herb that dies back in winter and re-emerges in early spring from a taproot.

directions: Honewort is a shade-loving native edible herb, native to moist, rich woodlands and forest edges throughout eastern North America from Quebec to Florida and west to Kansas, producing tender, edible leaves and stems in spring and early summer that taste mildly of parsley and celery — used raw in salads or cooked as a pot herb. It is closely related to Japanese mitsuba (Cryptotaenia japonica) and can substitute for it in East Asian cooking; the young leaves are particularly flavorful in early spring. A self-seeding perennial that naturalizes freely in the shade garden, it fills in the difficult deep-shade spaces where most vegetables fail, providing a continuous harvest of tender greens from early spring to summer heat. Harvest outer leaves regularly to maintain plant vigor; allow some plants to set seed for natural self-renewal.
sources: Edible Wild Plants of Eastern North America (Fernald & Kinsey)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before the last frost date; surface-sow on moist medium as seeds need light to germinate. Transplant to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade after frost danger has passed. Downy Lobelia re-seeds freely in favorable conditions.

directions: Downy Lobelia is a native perennial wildflower of the southeastern United States, native to moist woodlands, woodland edges, streambanks, and open meadows from New Jersey south to Florida and west to Texas, blooming from late summer to fall with dense spikes of bright blue-violet flowers that are excellent for attracting hummingbirds and bumblebees. Similar in appearance to the more widely cultivated Cardinal Flower (Lobelia cardinalis) and Great Blue Lobelia (Lobelia siphilitica), Downy Lobelia is distinguished by its downy-hairy stems and leaves and its preference for somewhat drier, better-drained soils than its relatives. A robust, short-lived perennial that perpetuates itself by self-seeding in the garden, it is a valuable late-season pollinator plant for naturalistic borders and woodland gardens in zones 5–9. Provide partial shade, moderately moist and slightly acidic soil, and allow seed heads to mature and disperse for natural re-seeding.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold stratify seeds for 30 days before sowing. Transplant to moist to slightly dry, rocky or sandy, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade after frost has passed.

directions: Heartleaf Skullcap is a shade-tolerant native perennial mint-family herb, native to dry to moist rocky woodlands, forest edges, and limestone bluff bases from Maryland and Ohio south to Florida and west to Texas, blooming from May to July with loose racemes of blue to violet flowers held above large, heart-shaped, opposite leaves. Unlike many skullcaps that prefer full sun, this species thrives in the dappled shade of open woodland and is one of the more shade-tolerant native salvias for the naturalistic shade garden. It provides excellent nectar for bumble bees and is a larval host plant for several native moth species. Grow in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade; it is notably drought-tolerant once established and performs better in drier shade than most native woodland wildflowers.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in late spring after all frost danger has passed, in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil at the margins of ponds, streams, or water gardens; Thalia dealbata tolerates water up to 12 inches deep over the crown and thrives in the wet, low-oxygen soils of bayous and marsh edges.

directions: Powdery Thalia is a bold, tropical-looking native marginal aquatic plant, native to the shallow waters, bayous, and marshy pond margins of the southeastern United States from South Carolina to Florida and west to Texas, producing large, blue-green, powdery (glaucous) leaves reminiscent of a giant canna on stems reaching 4–6 feet and small violet-purple flowers on tall, branched spikes in summer. It is one of the most architecturally impressive native aquatic plants for water gardens, rain gardens, and naturalized pond edges, providing year-round coarse texture in the shallow water zone and offering excellent cover and nesting habitat for ducks and wading birds. In zones 6–7 the crown may die to the waterline in severe winters but re-sprouts reliably from the roots in spring; in zones 8–11 it is evergreen or semi-evergreen. Plant rhizome divisions in still or slow-moving water at pond margins, in containers submerged 2–4 inches below the water surface, or in saturated soil at the water's edge.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in the saturated muddy soil at pond margins or submerged up to 12 inches in still to slow-moving water. Golden Club tolerates both shallow standing water and periodically dry pond margins.

directions: Golden Club is a striking native aquatic perennial, native to the still waters, bayous, and blackwater swamps of the eastern United States from Massachusetts south to Florida and west to Louisiana, producing distinctive golden-tipped white spadices (flower spikes) that emerge from the water in early spring before the leaves fully expand — one of the earliest aquatic plants to bloom and a valuable early pollinator resource. The large, velvety, blue-green elliptic leaves float on the water surface in deep water or stand erect in shallower zones, and the waxy leaf cuticle causes water to bead and roll off in perfect spheres — a phenomenon visible throughout the growing season. Easy to grow in full sun to partial shade in the marginal zone of water gardens, ponds, and rain gardens, Golden Club forms clumps that slowly expand by rhizome and requires minimal care once established. Use in naturalized pond margins alongside pickerelweed, blue flag iris, and lizard's tail for a layered native aquatic planting.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring, anchoring the thick horizontal rhizome in heavy clay or loamy pond-bottom soil at a depth of 1–4 feet of standing water. Yellow Pond Lily thrives in slow-moving streams, ponds, bayous, and blackwater swamps and should not be planted in fast-moving water.

directions: Yellow Pond Lily is a vigorous native floating-leaf aquatic plant, native to the slow ponds, sluggish streams, bayous, and blackwater swamps of eastern North America from New Brunswick to Florida and across the Gulf states to Texas, bearing large, heart-shaped floating leaves up to a foot across and distinctive globular yellow flowers on stout stems from spring through fall — the flowers are pollinated by beetles and tiny bees attracted to the scent and heat generated by the floral chamber. The thick, horizontal rhizomes anchor in muddy pond bottoms and can spread aggressively, making Yellow Pond Lily best suited to large natural ponds and water gardens where it can spread freely; its roots, seeds, and leaf bases are important food for wood ducks, beaver, and muskrats. Plant rhizomes in heavy clay soil at the pond bottom at a depth of 1–4 feet; once established, plants require no care and will spread naturally to cover the water surface, providing excellent shade that suppresses filamentous algae.
